					<description><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from Princeton University published a technical paper titled “Hardware Mechanisms to Dynamically Throttle AI Performance.” Abstract Excerpt: “In this paper, we introduce a set of microarchitecture knobs which dynamically control the available hardware resources to limit AI performance at runtime. We evaluate candidate knobs spanning the GPU memory subsystem, across capacity, bandwidth, latency and... <a class="read_more" href="https://semiengineering.com/hw-based-methods-to-dynamically-throttle-ai-performance-princeton-university/">&#187; read more</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/hw-based-methods-to-dynamically-throttle-ai-performance-princeton-university/">HW-based Methods to Dynamically Throttle AI Performance (Princeton University)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from Princeton University published a technical paper titled “Hardware Mechanisms to Dynamically Throttle AI Performance.”</p>
<p><strong>Abstract Excerpt:</strong></p>
<p>“In this paper, we introduce a set of microarchitecture knobs which dynamically control the available hardware resources to limit AI performance at runtime. We evaluate candidate knobs spanning the GPU memory subsystem, across capacity, bandwidth, latency and frequency dimensions, and narrow down to four strong candidates: L2 size, L2 latency, L2 bandwidth, and shared memory port access rate.&#8221;</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18069">here</a>. July 2026.</p>
<p>Ma, Haiyue, Lauren Malek, Joseph Forzani, and David Wentzlaff. “Hardware Mechanisms to Dynamically Throttle AI Performance.” arXiv, July 2026. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.18069.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/hw-based-methods-to-dynamically-throttle-ai-performance-princeton-university/">HW-based Methods to Dynamically Throttle AI Performance (Princeton University)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Researchers from imec, KU Leuven, Samsung Electronics, and Lam Research published a technical paper titled “Optimization of 3D-DRAM Architecture with Oxide-Semiconductor Channel through Process and Device Simulation.”</p>
<p><strong>Abstract Excerpt:</strong> “We investigate a monolithically stackable OSC-based 3D-DRAM cell using an integrated framework combining process emulation, TCAD device simulation, parasitic extraction, and analytical modeling.”</p>
<p>Find the technical paper <a href="https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/11614411">here</a>. July 2026.</p>
<p>J. Hong et al., &#8220;Optimization of 3D-DRAM Architecture with Oxide-Semiconductor Channel through Process and Device Simulation,&#8221; in IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society, doi: 10.1109/JEDS.2026.3715074. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/monolithic-3d-dram-with-oxide-semiconductor-architecture-imec-ku-leuven-samsung-lam/">Monolithic 3D-DRAM with Oxide-Semiconductor Architecture (imec, KU Leuven, Samsung, Lam)</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>

		<item>
		<title>Research Bits: July 21</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-21/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-21/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Jesse Allen]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Tue, 21 Jul 2026 07:01:14 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Research]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Research: LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[2D semiconductors]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Chalmers University of Technology]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[defect detection]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[defects]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[DEVCOM Army Research Laboratory]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[diamond]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[flexible electronics]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[inspection]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[KIMM]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Korea Institute of Machinery and Materials]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[lithography]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Rice University]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[roll-to-roll processing]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Stanford University]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[TMDs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[transition-metal dichalcogenides]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[wide bandgap semiconductors]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[X-ray diffraction]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24279079</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Thin TMD transistors; diamond defect detection; roll-to-roll litho.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-21/">Research Bits: July 21</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<h2>Thin TMD transistors</h2>
<p>Researchers from Chalmers University of Technology and Stanford University fabricated transistors from three different atomically thin <a href="https://news.cision.com/chalmers/r/extremely-thin-transistors-bring-future-energy-efficient-chips-a-step-closer,c4370972">2D semiconductors</a> with channel widths as small as 25nm.</p>
<p>“These are some of the slimmest high-performance transistors demonstrated in two-dimensional semiconductors,” said Anton Persson, assistant professor at Chalmers University of Technology, in a press release. “What is particularly encouraging, and actually surprised us, is that the transistors remained well-behaved even when shrunk to dimensions relevant for future industrial technologies. That has been a major uncertainty in the field.”</p>
<p>The nanoribbon transistors were fabricated from three different monolayer transition metal dichalcogenides: molybdenum disulfide, tungsten disulfide, and tungsten diselenide. To prevent tears or peeling during fabrication, the team developed a “dog-bone” structure with an extremely narrow transistor channel and wider regions under the electrical contacts to help anchor the material in place. An advanced multi-patterning technique that etched the material in two steps from different directions was also used.</p>
<p>“The 2D transistors made of tungsten disulfide were especially notable, as their current density improved by more than a hundred times compared to previous demonstrations, thanks to better material quality and improved metal contacts,” said Persson.</p>
<p>“Our findings don’t mean that atomically thin 2D semiconductors are ready to replace silicon tomorrow,” added Eric Pop, professor at Stanford University, in a press release. “But it shows that one of the key scaling concerns may be far less limiting than previously thought.” [1]</p>
<h2>Diamond defect detection</h2>
<p>Researchers from Rice University and DEVCOM Army Research Laboratory developed a custom Python-based software tool to rapidly analyze data from high-resolution X-ray diffraction and detect dislocation density <a href="https://news.rice.edu/news/2026/rice-researchers-automate-defect-detection-diamond-other-advanced-semiconductors">defects in diamond</a> and other wide-bandgap semiconductor materials. The software analyzes diffraction patterns, picks up on dislocations and irregularities in the atomic lattice, and calculates their density in a given material.</p>
<p>“Dislocations can disrupt how charge and heat move through the material, which impacts how efficient and reliable a device is, and how easy it is to manufacture at scale,” said Xiang Zhang, assistant research professor of materials science and nanoengineering at Rice, in a statement.</p>
<p>The framework was tested by analyzing four commercially available grades of single-crystal diamond with different expected levels of crystal quality. The automated workflow was able to distinguish among the materials, identifying electronic-grade diamond as having the lowest defect density and most uniform crystal quality. Heteroepitaxial diamond grown on a non-diamond substrate exhibited the highest defect density and greatest structural disorder.</p>
<p>The team also successfully applied the workflow to gallium nitride. Next, they plan to expand the range of materials and defect types it can analyze. [2]</p>
<h2>Roll-to-roll litho</h2>
<p>Researchers from the Korea Institute of Machinery and Materials (KIMM) developed a <a href="https://www.kimm.re.kr/eng/sub011001/view/id/1572">roll-to-roll maskless</a> digital lithography system for continuous patterning of flexible substrates with line widths below 10μm.</p>
<p>The system combines Digital Micromirror Device (DMD)-based exposure technology to selectively project UV light onto designated areas of a substrate. A dedicated exposure module and a high-precision web-transport control system enables continuous roll-to-roll operation, while vision-based measurements adjust the projected pattern in real time, compensating for positional errors and substrate deformation.</p>
<p>Because the patterns can be generated and modified through software control, the approach doesn&#8217;t need conventional photomasks and can support continuous, large-area manufacturing over long substrate lengths.</p>
<p>“The R2R digital lithography system is a key platform technology for the mass production of flexible electronic devices. We expect it to be used in a wide range of applications, including flexible printed circuit boards, high-resolution flexible electronics, and semiconductor packaging,” said Won Seok Chang, director of the Nano-convergence Manufacturing Research Division at KIMM, in a statement. “The technology can also be applied to digital exposure processes for patterning roll surfaces, further broadening its industrial applications.” [3]</p>
<h3>References</h3>
<p>[1] T. Peña, A.E.O. Persson, A. Krayev, et al. Scaling nanoribbon transistors with monolayer transition metal dichalcogenides. Nat. Nanotechnol. 21, 803–809 (2026). <a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41565-026-02161-w">https://doi.org/10.1038/s41565-026-02161-w</a></p>
<p>[2] T. Gray, E. J. Garratt, X. Zhang, et al. “High Throughput X-Ray Characterization of Defects in Wide-Bandgap Semiconductors.” Advanced Materials 38, no. 39 (2026): e73678. <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.73678">https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.73678</a></p>
<p>[3] J. Choi, W. S. Lee, G. Kim, et al. DMD-based adaptive lithography system for real-time substrate deformation compensation. International Journal of Optomechatronics, 20(1). (2026). <a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/15599612.2026.2635849">https://doi.org/10.1080/15599612.2026.2635849</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-21/">Research Bits: July 21</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

<p>Abstract Excerpt: “Here we present a robust approach for patterning of conformal thin-film coatings on complex 3D structures.” “It is agnostic to the used thin-film deposition process and enables even lift-off patterning of atomic layer deposited (ALD) conformal coatings, a task infeasible for conventional shadowing-based lift-off processes.”</p>

		<item>
		<title>Verifying Networks On Chip</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/verifying-networks-on-chip/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/verifying-networks-on-chip/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Ed Sperling]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Mon, 20 Jul 2026 07:15:30 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[System-Level Design]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Videos]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Axiomise]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[chiplets]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[coherency]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[data security]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[formal verification]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[serialization bugs]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[silent data corruption]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24279044</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Challenges increase significantly with chiplets and coherency.  </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/verifying-networks-on-chip/">Verifying Networks On Chip</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>More data, more processing elements, and more memories are making it much harder to maintain consistent throughput in a multi-die assembly. There is more concurrent traffic, more potential interactions, and more protocol layers. Ashish Darbari, CEO of Axiomise, talks about the of coherency on serialization bugs, how to ensure data is secure, and how to ferret out the root cause of problems like silent data corruption using formal technology.</p>
<p><iframe width="480" height="270" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/FZrYySrBLpA" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/verifying-networks-on-chip/">Verifying Networks On Chip</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>
